content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

June 25, 1991

Honorable Laura Pearson
City Councilmember, Ward 7
City of Riverside
3900 Main Street
Riverside, CA 92522

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
     Our File No. I-91-287

Dear Ms. Pearson:

     You have requested advice concerning your responsibilities as
a city councilmember for the City of Riverside under the Political
Reform Act (the "Act"). Since your advice request does not refer
to a specific governmental decision but seeks general guidance, we
are treating your request as one for informal assistance.

QUESTIONS

     1.   As a newly elected city councilmember, may you use your
campaign bank account established for your current term of office
to pay for a constituent newsletter?

     2.   What is the proper method for you to establish a future
campaign committee and campaign bank account under the Act?

     3.   What are the identification requirements applicable to a
constituent newsletter pursuant to the Act?


_______________
     Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

     Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs.
Section 18329(c)(3).)


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

Our File No. I-91-287
Page 2


CONCLUSIONS

     1.  Expenditures for your newsletter may be made from your
campaign bank account established for your current term of office,
so long as your newsletter will not refer to your candidacy or
future election and is not sent within three months prior to any
election for which you have filed a statement of intention to be a
candidate, a declaration of candidacy or nomination papers, or any
other documents necessary to be listed on the ballot as a
candidate for any state or local office.

     2.  You may redesignate your campaign bank account
established for your current term of office as your campaign bank
account for your future election.  Committees and accounts may be
redesignated even if the committee and account continue to hold
campaign funds or outstanding debts from the previous election.
The funds may be carried over to the future election account.

     3.  When a single candidate sends a mass mailing, the
candidate's name, street address, and city must be shown on the
outside of each piece of mail in 6-point type.

DISCUSSION

Campaign Bank Accounts

     In June 1988, Proposition 73 was adopted by the voters as
amendments to the Act.  Proposition 73 provided that contributions
to candidates for elective office must comply with new fiscal year
contribution limits as set forth in Sections 85301, 85303 and
85305.³  In addition, Section 85201 provides that all
contributions or loans made to a candidate, or to the candidate's
controlled committee shall be deposited in a single campaign bank
account, and all campaign expenditures must be made from the
appropriate campaign bank account.

     Thus, pursuant to the Proposition 73 amendments to the Act,
although a candidate may have numerous campaign bank accounts for
elective offices, the candidate must make all expenditures
associated with a specific election from the appropriate campaign
bank account established for that election.  Moreover, Section

_________________________
3    On September 25, 1990, in Service Employees International
Union, AFL-CIO, et al. v. Fair Political Practices Commission
(1990) 747 F.Supp. 580 ("SEIU") the United States District Court
invalidated portions of the act added by Proposition 73, including
all the contribution limitations in the Act based on fiscal years
(Sections 85301, 85302 and 85303) and the transfer ban of Section
85304 as used to support the fiscal year contribution limitations.
This decision became final on January 18, 1991.  Please note,
however, that the campaign bank account sections of Proposition 73
were not affected by the court's decision.  (Sections 85200 -
85202.)  Moreover, the decision in SEIU is being appealed.

Our File No. I-91-287
Page 3


85202(b) of Proposition 73 provides that contributions deposited
into the campaign account shall be used only for expenses
associated with the election of the candidate to the specific
office which the candidate intends to seek or expenses associated
with holding that office.

     You have asked whether campaign funds raised to win election
to your current term of office may be used to prepare and
distribute a constituent newsletter.  Regulation 18525 provides:

     (a)  An incumbent elected officer shall make
expenditures for the following campaign expenses
from the appropriate campaign bank account for
election to a future term of office:

          (1)  Payments for fundraising and
campaign strategy expenses for election to a
future term of office.

          (2)  Payments for mass mailings,
political advertising, opinion polls or
surveys, and other communications in
connection with election to a future term of
office.  For purposes of this section, a mass
mailing, political advertisement, opinion poll
or survey, or other communication shall be
considered "in connection with election to a
future term of office" if it makes reference
to the officer's future election or status as
a candidate for a future term of office, or if
it is made by an incumbent officer within 3
months prior to an election for which he or
she has filed any of the following:

               (A)  A statement of intention to be
a candidate for a specific office,
pursuant to Government Code Section
85200.

               (B)  A declaration of candidacy or
nomination papers, as specified in
Chapter 5 (commencing with Section 6400)
of Division 6 of the Elections Code.

               (C)  Any other documents necessary
to be listed on the ballot as a candidate
for any state or local office.

                    * * *

     (b)  An incumbent elected officer may make
expenditures for purposes not enumerated in
subdivision (a) from either the campaign bank

Our File No. I-91-287
Page 4


account established pursuant to Government Code
Section 85201 for election to the incumbent term of
office or from a campaign bank account established
pursuant to Government Code Section 85201 for
election to a future term of office. This section
shall not be construed to permit an incumbent
elected officer to make expenditures from any
campaign bank account for expenses other than those
associated with his or her election to the specific
office for which the account was established and
expenses associated with holding that office.

     Thus, expenditures for mass mailings and other communications
which make reference to your future election or status as a
candidate must be made from your future campaign bank account.
Moreover, expenditures for mass mailings circulated within three
months prior to an election for which you have filed a statement
of intention to be a candidate (Section 85200), a declaration of
candidacy or nomination papers, or any other documents necessary
to be listed on the ballot as a candidate for any state or local
office must also be made from your future election campaign bank
account.

     Since your newsletter will not refer to your candidacy or
future election and you are not three months from your reelection,
it would appear the payment for your newsletter is an officeholder
expenditure which may be made from your campaign account for your
current term of office. At the time you file forms for your
future election and when you are within three months of your
reelection, you may no longer use your officeholder account for
the expenditure. The expenditure for the mailer is not
prohibited, it just must be paid from your future campaign
account.

Redesignating Campaign Bank Accounts

     As discussed above, a recent court decision invalidated the
transfer ban insofar as it was applied to limit transfers of
campaign funds between a candidate's own controlled committees.
In light of the changes caused by the federal court order, on
January 14, 1991, the Commission began advising that a candidate


4    The SEIU decision did not invalidate the special election and
special runoff election contribution limits and transfer ban of
Proposition 73. Therefore, these restrictions remain operative in
both local and statewide special elections (Section 85305).
Moreover, Section 85304 continues to prohibit candidates for state
or local office from transferring campaign funds to any candidate
who is subject to any valid contribution limits, including those
limits established by local jurisdictions.

Our File No. I-91-287
Page 5


may redesignate a campaign committee and campaign bank account
established after January 1, 1989, for reelection to the same
office at a future date.

     Consequently, the campaign bank account you used in your
campaign for election to your current term of office may be
redesignated as a future election account.  This would be
accomplished by filing a new candidate intention statement (Form
501) for the future election, and by amending the existing
committee's campaign bank account statement (Form 502) and
statement of organization (Form 410).  A committee may be
redesignated even if the committee and account continue to hold
campaign funds or outstanding debts from the previous election.5

Sender Identification

     Section 84305 provides:

     (a)  Except as provided in subdivision (b), no
candidate or committee shall send a mass mailing
unless the name, street address, and city of the
candidate or committee are shown on the outside of
each piece of mail in the mass mailing and on
at least one of the inserts included within each piece
of mail of the mailing in no less than 6-point type
which shall be in a color or print which contrasts
with the background so as to be easily legible.  A
post office box may be stated in lieu of a street
address if the organization's address is a matter
of public record with the Secretary of State.

     (b)  If the sender of the mass mailing is a
single candidate or committee, the name, street
address, and city of the candidate or committee
need only be shown on the outside of each piece of
mail.

     (c)  If the sender of a mass mailing is a
controlled committee, the name of the person
controlling the committee shall be included in
addition to the information required by subdivision
(a).

                              [Emphasis added.]

     A mass mailing is defined as over two hundred substantially
similar pieces of mail.  (Section 82041.5.)  Thus, according to


_____________________________
5    Regulation 18525(b) permits officeholder expenditures and
future-election expenditures to be made from the future election
campaign bank account.

Our File No. I-91-287
Page 6


Section 84305, when a single candidate sends a mass mailing, the
candidate's name, or the name of the committee where the committee
name includes the candidate's name, the street address, and city
must be shown on the outside of each piece of mail in 6-point
type. No identification is required inside the newsletter and the
name of your treasurer need not be noted in the mailing.

     If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Scott Hallabrin
                                        Acting General Counsel

                                   By:  John W. Wallace
                                        Counsel, Legal Division

SH:JWW:aa
